Finding the Balance: What I Learned from the "Direct-to-Client" Leap

​Three weeks into my job search, I’ve had some time to reflect on a major career pivot. After a successful tenure at TaskUs—where I was recognized as a Q2 Top 1% Team Leader, I decided to take a leap.

​Driven by the need for better financial security, I left the structured world of BPO management to work directly with clients as a VA. It was a calculated risk that paid off for five months until the business shifted, and our entire team was unexpectedly terminated.

​That’s the reality of the "direct-to-client" world: the highs are high, but the stability can be fragile.

​Resilience is Built on Terms. ​The biggest takeaway from this experience isn't that the risk was wrong, but that the strategy needed more layers. When you transition from a stable corporate leadership role to independent contracting, your mindset must shift from "Employee" to "Business Owner."

​Negotiate Harder on Notice Periods: In the corporate world, notice periods are standard. In the VA world, you have to fight for them in your contract to give yourself a safety net.

​I leaned into the role, but I should have been building a "Back-up Plan B" from day one—keeping my network active even when the work was steady.

​In my recent roles, I’ve seen the rising tide of AI. While some fear it, my time in operations has taught me the truth: AI is a tool, not a replacement.

​It takes a human to provide the strategy, the empathy, and the precise prompting to turn a raw AI output into a high-quality result. AI was created by humans to enhance our capabilities, but it still requires a human hand to guide it toward truly "better" results.

​I’m now looking for my next challenge where I can bring my leadership experience and my "human-in-the-loop" approach to AI operations. 

If your team is looking for a leader who understands both high-level management and the grit of independent contracting, let’s connect!

​#CareerPivot #Leadership #BPO #VirtualAssistant #AI #WorkLifeLessons #Hiring

A software company hired a senior architect on a permanent basis for a twelve-month transformation project.

Nine months in, the project scope changed.

The architecture decisions made in month two were no longer aligned to the new direction. The hire — excellent at what he was brought in to do — had no mandate to pivot. Letting him go cost three months of notice, severance, and an uncomfortable conversation that nobody wanted to have.

The total cost of that misalignment — in salary, severance, delayed delivery, and the re-hiring process that followed — was almost double what a contract engagement would have cost for the same period.

This is not an unusual story. It plays out regularly in technology teams where the hiring model hasn't kept pace with the project model.

Modern technology delivery is milestone-driven, not calendar-driven. Skill requirements shift with each phase. The team you need to architect a platform is not the same team you need to build it, test it, and operate it.

i-Qode Digital Solutions contract hiring practice gives companies access to pre-vetted, senior technical professionals — across DevOps, Cloud, AI, Web, and Mobile — on engagement structures that match the actual duration and shape of the work.

No permanent overhead for temporary needs. No capability gaps when the project demands a profile you don't have internally.

No severance conversations when the engagement concludes.

The right talent, for the right duration, with the right exit.

👉 www.i-qode.com | info@i-qode.com

CIOs: Your biggest leadership challenge isn’t technology, it’s managing a non-human workforce. 🤖

Here’s why AI is no longer just software, it’s your next team member:
1️⃣ AI as workforce, not tool 
→ Today: AI writes 45% of code (Gartner) and handles 20-40% of tech tasks.
→ 2027: 97% of HR leaders expect human-AI collaboration as the norm (CIO & Leader).
→ Implication: You’re not just deploying AI; you’re hiring, training, and governing it.
2️⃣ Agentic Governance: The new CIO mandate 
→ Problem: Traditional IT governance (policies, controls) fails for autonomous AI agents. 
→ Solution: Agentic Governance = rules + real-time monitoring + ethical guardrails. 
→ Example: AI agents negotiating contracts need bias audits and decision logs.
3️⃣ The upskilling paradox 
→ Myth: AI replaces jobs. 
→ Reality: AI transforms jobs, your team needs new skills to collaborate with agents. 
→ Action: Train employees to delegate to AI (e.g., "Prompt engineering for managers").

How to lead this shift: 
✅ Audit your AI workforce: Map where agents are already operating (e.g., chatbots, code gen). 
✅ Design governance frameworks: Define autonomy levels (e.g., "Agent can approve 10K€ purchases"). 
✅ Pilot hybrid teams: Pair humans + AI on high-impact projects (e.g., fraud detection).

Question: Is your organization treating AI as a tool… or a virtual colleague?

At Miranki, we help CIOs: 
→ Transition to Agentic Governance with frameworks for autonomous AI 
→ Upskill teams for human-AI collaboration 
→ Audit AI risks before they become liabilities

#AgenticGovernance #FutureOfWork #CIO

The traditional entry-level job is is on life-support, and nobody is talking about how to fix it.

The genuine reality for young professionals right now is that companies are reducing junior hiring because one AI tool can now do the grunt work that used to serve as the training ground for fresh grads. The junior squeeze is real.

So, how do you stand out when the very tasks you were supposed to cut your teeth on are being automated?

You have to skip the grunt work entirely.

The new junior reality isn't about how fast you can build a spreadsheet or draft a boilerplate contract. It is about curiosity and coordination. You need to become an AI orchestrator (I don't really like this term, but it seems to be what everyone has landed on).

As a junior, your value is no longer in doing the entry-level work; your value is in managing the AI agents that are doing the work, and ensuring their output actually aligns with the business goals.

If you want to get hired in 2026, stop selling your ability to do the task.
Start selling your ability to coordinate the tools.

When Google went public in 2004 it traded at 10x trailing revenue with revenue growth of 240% a year (and the market thought it was expensive).

SpaceX is going public at 125x trailing revenue with revenue growth of 25%, which means growth almost 10 times lower and a valuation more than 10 times higher.

Most analysts look at that and see a rocket company that is wildly overpriced, and if all SpaceX ever does is launch things into orbit and sell broadband they are probably right. But I think the more interesting question is whether SpaceX is building something closer to a railroad than a rocket company. The transcontinental railroad was not valued on ticket sales because the value was in everything that moved on the rails, the freight and the commerce and the entire cities that did not exist before the tracks were laid. The railroad created the American economy, it did not just connect it.

SpaceX could be building the railroad to space. McKinsey and the World Economic Forum project the space economy will grow from $630 billion today to $1.8 trillion by 2035, which is semiconductor-level growth at roughly double the pace of global GDP.

Part of the reason the valuation is so hard to judge is that this is not one company but at least five bundled together. Starlink with 10 million broadband subscribers, Falcon 9 which handles roughly 60-70% of all global launches, Starship which is still pre-revenue, a defense and government contract business with a $22 billion backlog, and xAI which is burning a billion dollars a month building AI infrastructure. Each of these would be valued completely differently as a standalone business but they are going public as one entity at one price.

I work with autonomy in trucks and mines and the pattern is always the same, the technology works but the constraint is labor. Try hiring operators for a remote mine site in northern Sweden and you understand why lunar mining will be 100% autonomous from day one. Starlink for communication, Starship for logistics, autonomous systems for everything else.

The railroad analogy might be right. But Google at 10x revenue and 240% growth turned out to be one of the best investments in history, and SpaceX at 125x and 25% is asking you to pay for the full journey before the tracks are even laid.

The company might be worth it. The price already assumes it is.

A Real Experience with Gemini: Are We Using AI for Real Business?

LinkedIn is saturated with gurus promising magic "prompts" for a few likes. But in the demanding world of editorial direction and Massive Collections management, we don't need magic; we need capability, reliability, and applied judgment.

My real-world experience with Gemini is not a set of tricks; it is the integration of a high-level strategic partner into a complex professional ecosystem. While others claim "Gemini cannot do X," I use it to:

Orchestrate Massive Logistics: Coordinate the simultaneous production of many titles while integrating inventory data, international POD, and delivery schedules, without losing strategic focus.

Integrate the Professional Ecosystem: Trace distribution agreements in legacy emails and connect them with technical specifications in Drive files to validate business proposals.

Perform Risk Analysis: Act as an honest consultant, dismantling weak reasoning and detecting blind spots in revenue strategies before presenting to partners.

Crosswalk Ethical Codes: Analyze mandates and ancient wisdom for humanities and religion editors, translating them into applicable ethical guidelines for professional and daily life.

Refine Editorial Contracts: Review every clause to protect ethics and mutual benefit, ensuring human quality over blind automation.

Design Localization Strategies: Adapt concepts (not just substitute words) to ensure a book collection works equally well in Mexico, Spain, or the Caribbean.

Structure Technical Support from Sources: Address user knowledge gaps on app logic, software functionality, and Android ecosystem hardware principles, based exclusively on manufacturer and official expert instructions.

Audit Creative Workflows (Design Apps/CRM): Define precise Editorial guidelines that reduce creative team correction times through technical audits without reliance on specific brands.

Protect Brand Voice: Maintain consistency in tone, purpose, and business vision across months of consecutive publications.

Exercise Judgment of Convenience: Act as a criterion amplifier that balances creative expansion with the necessary financial rigor.

AI is not the end; it is the tool that a Director with a proven track record and sufficient practical experience, matured over time, guides to achieve results. If technology is to be great, it must be guided by the greatness of judgment.

Are you hiring a prompt "guru," or the strategic intelligence that knows how to direct the technology?

No, you don't. You just have a ChatGPT tab open.

Someone is still pasting that data into ChatGPT, begging it to "make sense of this."
Someone is still formatting that output into a client deck.

You didn't build a system. You just added a tool to a broken process.

The "AI-Native" Agency:
→ Data pulls, analysis, and reporting happen while the team sleeps.
→ Onboarding triggers the moment the contract is signed.
→ New client = Pure profit.

Same tools. Completely different business models.
One scales with people. The other scales with systems.

AI won't save your agency. Rebuilding around it will.

Happy Automating.

Another year. Another chapter. Another reason to bow.🌱

Today is my birthday — and I am not celebrating quietly. Because this year has not been quiet.

First of all, This is my first birthday as a mother. Nothing recalibrates your purpose like a life that looks at you and sees the whole world.

Ten years ago, I started Oeuvre with nothing but conviction — no blueprint, no playbook, no guarantee. Just a stubborn belief that Africa deserved world-class events. A decade later, the clients who trusted us early are the reason I am standing here today.

To every client who chose Oeuvre — thank you. You funded a vision.

But God doesn’t let you settle.

On March 15, 2026, we launched Sinnapi  — a global SaaS marketplace and operations platform for the events industry. Built from 10 years of real experience and vendor relationships.

Within three weeks, we closed our first institutional partnership and facilitated over UGX 300M in bookings. We are just getting started.

🌍 The events industry is at an inflection point. AI and automation are rewriting the rules. Platforms that treat vendors as listings will lose. Platforms that treat vendors as businesses — with escrow, contracts, analytics, and discovery — will win. Africa is the growth frontier. Whoever builds the trust infrastructure first will define the next decade.

We are building that.

We’ll be at TechCrunch Disrupt in San Francisco this October — come find us.

📣 Planning an event? Start on Sinnapi. Book vendors, manage operations, pay securely.

🤝 We are hiring Sales Agents across Uganda and beyond. DM me.

Ebenezer — To God be all the glory
He has been faithful. He is faithful. He will be faithful.

I lost ₹4 lakhs trying to save ₹40,000.



I remembered 2 years back I needed a team for my next project.

But instead of hiring right, I hired cheap.


“Why pay more when someone can do the same work for less?”

+ AI 🤖 

That was my logic.

The first hire missed deadlines.

Client deliverables were late.

I was redoing work I was paying someone else to do.

The second hire disappeared after 3 weeks.

Mid-project. 
No handover. 
Nothing.

The third hire was good but by then I had already lost two clients who didn’t want to wait for me to “figure out my team.”

₹4 lakhs gone.

In refunds. 
Redoing work. 
Lost contracts. 
Wasted onboarding time.

All because I was protecting ₹40,000.

Here’s what I know now:

Cheap is never cheap.

You just pay differently.

With time. 
With clients. 
With reputation.

The most expensive person on your team is the wrong one.

Hire for the outcome you want.

Not the invoice you’re comfortable with.


Akash Anand  




#foundermistakes #startup #businessconsultant

The "Talent Drain" Paradox: Why AI Cannot Replace the 20 Years of Experience You’re Losing

As corporate strategists, we have spent the last three years obsessed with one metric: Automation potential. We’ve read the reports, crunched the numbers on efficiency gains, and projected the ROI of AI agents.

But in our rush to optimize, we have missed a ticking time bomb. We are currently witnessing a "Silent Drain"; the hemorrhaging of mid-career institutional knowledge; just as the data suggests we are not ready to replace the tacit wisdom these people carry.

The timeline of disruption is accelerating faster than our adoption curves.

Back in 2023, OpenAI predicted it would take a decade for AI to significantly impact the workforce . By 2026, Cognizant updated that forecast dramatically: the "velocity score" of AI adoption has jumped from 2% to 9% annually. We are now compressing a decade of change into just two years .

But here is the strategic trap I see across Finance, Tech, and Legal sectors:

1. The "Entry-Level" Cliff (The 2024-2026 Trend)
Data from Stanford’s Digital Economy Lab (analyzing 285M job ads) shows that AI-exposed industries have seen an 18%-40% reduction in entry-level hiring . We stopped hiring juniors because AI can write the first draft of code or the first pass of a contract.

The Consequence: We have closed the "farm system." In 5 years, we will have no senior managers, because we didn't train any.

2. The "Middle Manager" Liquidation (The 2026 Reality)
We assumed AI would hit repetitive tasks first. It didn’t. It hit coordination. C-suites are realizing that AI agents (Agentic AI) can handle scheduling, basic resource allocation, and report generation. We saw layoffs at Microsoft, UPS, and Comcast specifically targeting management layers in late 2025 .

The Consequence: We are cutting the very people who hold the tacit knowledge; the context of why the 2018 merger failed, or how to handle the irregular client exception.

3. The Great Inversion (PwC Data)
While we freeze generalist hiring, demand for "AI Hybrid" workers has exploded 3x. PwC data shows a 15-30% wage premium for the same job if you add an AI skill .

The Strategic Warning:
We are suffering from a Talent Drain not because people are leaving, but because we are failing to let them evolve. By firing the mid-level manager to save costs and asking a junior (or an LLM) to replace them, we are forgetting that strategy requires judgment. AI provides synthesis; it does not provide accountability.

The Verdict:
If you hollow out your middle class of talent today to balance a spreadsheet, you will have no one left to train the AI or verify the output in 2027. The AI is the engine, but the institutional memory is the steering wheel.

Question for the network:
Are we over-indexing on the cost savings of AI and under-indexing on the cost of losing 5-10 years of industry-specific human memory?

There are jobs on LinkedIn… you’ve just been searching the wrong way.



this is exactly what happened to me:

it got me a response within twenty four hours.

two days later, I was assessed.
after that, an interview was scheduled.



so instead of applying randomly…

this is the exact search method I used:

"Talent Sourcer" OR "Sourcing Specialist"
AND (contract OR freelance)
AND (HR OR Recruiter)
AND (hiring OR vacancy)
site:linkedin.com &tbs=qdr:w


it looks technical, but it is actually simple.

let me break it down:

"Talent Sourcer" OR "Sourcing Specialist" means the role you are targeting

(contract OR freelance) means the type of job you want

(hiring OR vacancy) shows that they are actively looking for someone

site:linkedin.com means the results will come from LinkedIn

&tbs=qdr:w means results from the past week (very important)



now here is what you should do:

STEP 1: copy the entire search string.

paste it into ChatGPT or any AI tool and say:

“adjust this boolean search string for my role: [insert your role]”

for example:

social media manager
data analyst
medical virtual assistant



STEP 2: then take the new version…

paste it into Google and go through the results carefully.

look for:

1. recent postings
2. active recruiters
3. roles that match what you actually want



STEP 3: then do what most people avoid…

send a direct message.

keep it short, clear, and straight to the point.

attach your CV immediately.

All About US Agencies Hiring Asians 

Want to get remote work for US businesses/agencies? Read this first 👇

💼 Types of Remote Jobs:

• Full-time (35–40 hrs/week, Mon–Fri)
• Part-time (17–20 hrs/week)
• Freelance (flexible, up to ~10 hrs/week)
• Contract, Temporary & Permanent
➡️ Most roles are paid hourly

💰 Salary Expectations:

• Asians should target: $5–15/hour
• Full-time earning potential: PKR 250K–600K/month
• Higher-paying roles are often reserved for U.S. nationals
• U.S. companies hire from Pakistan, India & Philippines to reduce costs → BIG opportunity

🏢 Types of Companies Hiring:

Marketing Agencies

• Teams of 10–50 people
• Clear hierarchy (Executives → Managers → Leads → CEO)
• You handle your role & delegate the rest
• Stable structure but risk if agency loses clients
• Example: Working in agencies firms gives strong exposure

I worked at No Joke Marketing and Raxxar Marketing Group. 

Direct U.S. Businesses

• Small teams (2–3 people per project)
• Direct communication with business owner
• More control + better growth opportunities
• More secure (not dependent on one client/agency)

I worked/working with JDJ Consulting Group, AI for CRE, Hylton Electric, Warren and Migliaccio, Interlet

Talent Sourcing Agencies

• Complex structure, multiple layers
• No direct client communication
• Tasks go through managers → slower process
• Risk if assigned client is lost.

I got roles in a few of these, but I prefer project based now instead of full time. 

📊 Pay Structure by Role:

• Executives: $5–8/hour
• Managers: $8–12/hour
• Team Leads: $12+/hour
➡️ Freelance = higher hourly rate
➡️ Full-time = higher monthly income (due to more hours)

📄 Types of Contracts:

Employee (Full-time/Part-time)
• Paid leaves + paid breaks
• Fixed schedule (aligned with US time zones: EST/PST/CST)
• Usually 4 PM – 12 AM / up to 2 AM (PKT)
• Requires time tracking tools:
– Screenshots
– Activity monitoring
– Productivity tracking
• Regular audio/video meetings

Independent Contractor (Freelance)

• No time tracking
• Higher hourly rates
• No paid leaves/benefits
• Flexible working hours

💳 Payment Cycle:
• Weekly (7 days) or bi-weekly (15 days)
• Payments are consistent & reliable

🚀 **The Future of Hiring: Open Source Job Portals Powered by AI**

click , do , submit get paid. 
**open source collaboration?**

---

💡 Inspired by the rise of powerful open-source AI models like:

* LLaMA
* Mistral
* Gemma
* Qwen

…we can rethink how work itself is distributed and evaluated.

---

🌐 **Introducing the Concept: Open Source Job Portal**

A platform where:

🏢 **Companies (or even individuals)**
– Post real work instead of job descriptions
– Define expected outcomes, timelines, and budgets

👨‍💻 **Talent (anyone, anywhere)**
– Picks up the task
– Submits actual work (not just claims on a resume)

🤖 **AI Evaluation Engine**
– Automatically reviews submissions
– Matches output with defined criteria
– Scores quality, efficiency, and creativity

⏳ **After deadline closes**
– Multiple submissions are compared
– AI ranks the best outcomes objectively

💰 **Instant Outcome-Based Payment**
– Best submission wins
– Payment is automatically released
– No bias. No delay. No negotiation games.

---

🔥 **Why this model changes everything**

* Skills > Degrees
* Output > Interviews
* Meritocracy > Networking advantage
* Speed > Hiring cycles

---

⚠️ **Challenges to solve**

* Fair evaluation metrics
* Preventing plagiarism / AI misuse
* Ensuring transparency in scoring
* Legal & contract frameworks

---

🌱 **The Big Shift**

From: *“Hire first, then test”*
To: **“Test first, then reward”**

---

📢 The question is no longer *“Who is qualified?”*
It becomes:

👉 **“Who can actually deliver?”**

---

"War in the Middle East has given US companies a headache that extends well beyond dealing with higher energy costs, the US Federal Reserve said.

'The conflict in the Middle East was cited as a major source of uncertainty that complicated decision-making around hiring, pricing and capital investment, with many firms adopting a wait-and-see posture,' the central bank reported in its Beige Book survey of regional business contacts released on Wednesday [15 April 2026].

Despite that higher uncertainty, economic activity continued to increase at a slight-to-modest pace across most US regions, the Fed said.

Price growth remained moderate overall, but energy and fuel costs leapt in all 12 Fed districts. The report also noted that price pressures were bleeding beyond energy.

'Energy and fuel costs rose sharply in all districts, attributed to the Middle East conflict, leading to higher freight and shipping costs and higher prices for plastics, fertilisers and other petroleum-based products,' the Fed said. 'Input cost pressures beyond energy-related increases were also widespread.'

The oil shock spurred by the conflict has sent petrol prices in the US to their highest level since 2022, leading US inflation to jump in March.

The report featured information compiled by the New York Fed and collected till Apr 6.
...
On the employment side, the US labour market remained stable across most districts. The report added, however, that 'several districts noted increased demand for temporary or contract workers, as firms remained cautious about committing to permanent hires'.

A separate government report released earlier this month showed US job growth rebounded in March and the unemployment rate fell, pointing to some stabilisation in the labour market as the war with Iran began.

Some firms surveyed for the Beige Book said that artificial intelligence-driven productivity gains had made it possible to delay or reduce hiring. Most districts reported, however, that AI had not yet meaningfully affected overall staffing levels."

Jonnelle Marte, War’s Impact on Firms Goes Beyond Energy Costs: Fed’s Beige Book, 𝘉𝘭𝘰𝘰𝘮𝘣𝘦𝘳𝘨 𝘕𝘦𝘸𝘴, 15 April 2026, https://lnkd.in/gdXA2arq

Oracle is eliminating up to 30,000 positions to redirect capital — $8–10 billion — into AI infrastructure buildout. Alphabet has committed $175–185 billion in AI capex for 2026 alone. Across the US, AI data centers are expanding to 4,000+ sites, drawing both massive investment and serious community backlash.

The scale of this moment is hard to overstate.

But here's a strategic blind spot I keep seeing among small and mid-sized business owners:

"AI is built for enterprises. It's too expensive and too complex for us."

This is demonstrably false — and the hyperscale investment happening right now is exactly why.

When Oracle and Alphabet pour hundreds of billions into AI infrastructure, the downstream effect is commoditization. Compute costs drop. APIs become cheaper. AI-powered tools that once required six-figure contracts are now accessible via monthly SaaS pricing.

Practical example: AI-powered receptionist and call-handling technology. Two years ago, this was a custom enterprise build. Today, small businesses can deploy a 24/7 AI receptionist — one that answers calls, qualifies leads, and books appointments — without hiring additional staff or managing complex systems.

The infrastructure arms race between tech giants isn't happening in a vacuum. It's building the foundation that makes AI accessible at every business tier.

The real competitive risk for small businesses in 2026 isn't that AI is too expensive. It's that competitors are already using it while others are still debating whether it's "relevant" to them.

If you're rethinking your front-desk or call-handling operations, this is worth exploring: https://lnkd.in/eaW-Pa8R

9 things every CEO should have their CFO (or Fractional CFO) doing with AI this month:
Run a 30-minute health check on Q1 actuals before the team builds the reforecast. Let your Fractional CFO use AI to surface hidden issues early.
Stress-test the one assumption that keeps you up at night. Have your CFO run the AI analysis first — no model changes yet, just clear insight.
Ask your Fractional CFO to build a first-draft board deck from last quarter’s actuals — six slides max — then refine it together with AI.
Have your CFO upload the last 3 board decks and use AI to reveal: “What story has our board been telling itself about the business?” The patterns will surprise both of you.
Drop your customer concentration data in and have your Fractional CFO ask AI: “What’s the worst-case scenario if we lose our top 3 customers in the next 12 months?”
Upload key vendor contracts and let your CFO use AI to instantly flag every auto-renewal hitting in the next 90 days.
Build a one-page hiring brief for your next finance role that includes “proven AI workflow expertise” as a required interview question — your Fractional CFO can draft this in minutes.
Have your CFO run a sentiment analysis on the last earnings call transcript and highlight the exact moments your tone shifted — and what it means.
Ask your Fractional CFO to have AI draft a candid memo to you: “What would we do differently in the next 6 months if we suddenly had only 50% of today’s budget?”
Nine high-impact workflows. Roughly two hours total when your CFO or Fractional CFO leads them with AI.
Each one delivers insights that can save or make far more time and money within the next week.
As a CEO, you don’t need to master every AI tool yourself. The best move is having a sharp Fractional CFO (or full-time CFO) who leverages AI as a force multiplier — giving you faster, clearer, more objective financial intelligence while you stay focused on vision, customers, and growth.
The smartest CEOs I know are pairing world-class fractional talent with AI to stay ahead without bloating headcount.

Chicago IT hiring is tighter than it looks — here's what the numbers say...

I know we are waiting for some key market numbers on the overall labor force.

From what I have seen thus far Chicago ranks in the top 5 US metros for tech talent concentration.  But don't let that fool you.

For specialized roles — cloud architects, data engineers, cybersecurity analysts — the talent pool in Chicagoland is genuinely thin.

What I'm seeing right now:
📌 Java/Spring developers: 3–4 competing offers common
📌 Cloud (AWS/Azure): Candidates often have 2+ opportunities in hand
📌Cybersecurity: Demand is outpacing supply across every vertical
📌 Project Managers with BFSI experience: Extremely limited bench

** I am not mentioning the demand in AI related roles as this is certainly the buzz within the industry but everyone has a different take and stance on how it is impacting their organization and how to properly incorporate it**

The companies winning? They move fast.   Speed and efficiency are generally winning the war of the talent pool!bThey don't do 5-round interviews for contract roles. 

They treat candidates like people, not applicants.Slow process = lost candidate. Every time.  What's your biggest hiring bottleneck right now?

Slack was considered one of the fastest-growing enterprise software companies ever, and it still took years to become mainstream.

AI is moving faster than cloud.

Faster than SaaS.

Faster than mobile.

Why we are not building another AI Copilot
For the past few years, my team has built AI data centers across Taiwan, Japan, and North America — pushing PUE under 1.10. But infrastructure is not what I want to talk about today.
The question I keep hearing from CIO friends: why does serious AI investment so often end up feeling like a more expensive search engine?
They deploy Microsoft Copilot. Two weeks later, employees quietly go back to ChatGPT — because the corporate tool does not understand their workflow, does not remember their preferences, and does not know their compliance constraints.
Deploying a general-purpose LLM into an enterprise is like hiring a brilliant new graduate and immediately asking them to run a board meeting. The intelligence is there. The context is not.
The problem is not the model. The architecture is wrong. A real enterprise AI operating system has to solve three things:
One, every employee needs their own AI partner, not a shared chatbot. Legal's AI understands contracts. Procurement's AI remembers suppliers. CEO's AI reads KPI trends.
Two, every AI decision must be auditable. When AI makes decisions on your behalf, you have to answer the auditor: "Why did it recommend that?" We treat this the way aviation treats a flight recorder — every decision leaves a trace that cannot be tampered with.
Three, AI should let you simulate outcomes before committing. A good AI system behaves more like a flight simulator than a calculator — rehearse the scenario before you fly the plane.
This is why we built CORA. Not a chatbot. The cognitive nervous system of an organization.
We do not describe what CORA does in demos. We let customers attack it. Throw prompt injections. Upload database silos and watch GraphRAG build a knowledge graph in seconds. Simulate 100+ virtual employees facing a supply chain crisis.
Not a video. Real AI making decisions, live.
If you have been wondering why your enterprise AI investment is not producing the ROI the slides promised, I would be glad to compare notes.
#EnterpriseAI #AGI #CORA #CitruxAI

Everybody is worried about AI taking over their jobs. But everyone is worried about the wrong people losing their jobs to AI - as per an MIT study "Iceberg Index". And the workers most at risk are not who you'd expect.

For the last 2-3 years, the narrative has been that - "software developers are getting laid off"... "junior devs will never find work".... "tech companies are freezing hiring."

While this is real, the new MIT study says, it is only "the tip of the iceberg" (hence the appropriately named study, the Iceberg Index). The MIT study says this is only 1/5th of the actual problem. 

The MIT study, instead of focusing on answering "which jobs can AI replace" focused on "which tasks inside jobs can AI perform". 

And here's the scary thing that they found:

1. People in white-collar jobs, with degrees and careers built around reading, writing, analyzing, and summarizing, are the most exposed. 

2. Everyone talks about tech jobs being lost, which is 2.2% of the US labor market, and is typically the tip of the iceberg

3. Total economy's exposure (the stuff under the tip of the iceberg) is around 11.7% or nearly $1.2 trillion. 

Importantly, AI isn't replacing jobs overnight. It's quietly compressing the tasks inside them. A lawyer doesn't disappear overnight, but the hours spent on routing contract review shrink. The disruption doesn't show up in unemployment data until it's already well underway. 

It is an important study worth a read (or at least have an AI summarize it to make it easier to read).

The link is in the comments.

📊 USA & Canada Job Market Update – 2026

The job market in North America is going through an interesting phase right now 👇

🔹 Hiring is stable but slow
Many companies are in a “low-hire, low-fire” mode — maintaining teams instead of aggressively expanding.

🔹 Tech layoffs vs demand gap
While layoffs are still happening in tech, companies are struggling to find skilled talent in areas like AI, cloud, and cybersecurity.

🔹 Contract hiring is rising
More companies are shifting toward contract / C2C hiring to stay flexible in uncertain market conditions.

🔹 Canada market showing mixed signals
Recent job losses have raised concerns, but demand for specialized tech roles continues to grow.

This is how many “decision systems” still work.

People who committed similar crimes received sentences ranging from 15 days to 15 years, depending on which judge handled the case.
The same thing happens in B2B revenue.

Two reps. Same deal. Same context.
Different pricing. Different concessions. Different outcomes.
In his bestselling book Noise, Daniel Kahneman calls this variability in judgment.
Same inputs. Different decisions.

We’ve built systems to support decisions. CRMs, pricing tools, approval workflows, AI copilots. But not to make them consistent.

So outcomes don’t scale. Variability does.

At Pareto Agent, we’re taking a different approach.
NOT assisting judgment. ENCODING it.

Naive LLM approaches don’t fix this. They introduce a new kind of variability.
• inconsistent pricing logic
• confident outputs that ignore constraints

You’re not removing variance. You’re replacing human variability with stochastic variability. What you need is control.

An agentic system where:
• domain specialists reason over specific dimensions of a deal
• policies and constraints govern every action
• outputs are auditable, repeatable, and economically correct

That’s what we’re building. Systems that negotiate real contracts, protect real revenue, and operate within real-world constraints. Engineering quality matters.
It’s the difference between an AI that closes deals and one that gives away margin.

We’re at the stage where decisions made in the next 12 months will define the architecture for years. If you’ve ever wanted to design something foundational, not inherit it, this is that moment.

WE’RE HIRING

Engineers who want early ownership over hard problems, working alongside repeat founders building from first principles.

DM me or visit the link in the comments.

Something shifted in the last 12 months that most PMs haven't caught up to yet.

CEOs are coding.
Sundar Pichai submitted a pull request at Google in 2026. 
Satya Nadella built a live app on stage at Microsoft Build.

If CEOs are doing it - your manager is expected to. 
And if your manager is expected to… you see where this is going.

In Friday's session, we walked through exactly what this means for PMs trying to break into AI roles, and what hiring managers are actually testing for now in technical interviews.

A few things that came up that I haven't seen talked about enough:
→ The difference between vibe coding and velocity coding (they're not the same thing, and confusing them in an interview will cost you)
→ Why most PMs fail the vibe coding interview - not because they can't build, but because of one specific mistake they make in the first 5 minutes
→ The 4-layer skill stack that separates a regular PM from a Builder PM in 2026
→ A live contract analysis case study walkthrough - including the exact questions you should ask before touching any tool

We also stayed back for Q&A and some of the best questions came at the end - 
"Can I start with a small prompt and build up?" 
"Do interviewers care which tool you use?" 
"How do you track hallucination inside a prototype?"
All answered. All in the write-up.

If you're subscribed to the Substack - check your inbox. Full session breakdown is there, including the Q&A and a lab you can work through yourself.

I spoke to some post-secondary students recently about the job market. Parts one through three covered the market reality, how hiring actually works, and how to build a resume and portfolio that gets noticed.

Part four is about improving your odds and what to do once you're in.
You're one in 250. Here's how to improve those odds.

1: Network, but do it properly. Not transactionally. Two-way. Connect with people, learn from them, share things. Give before you ask.

In real life: go to events, be awkward. I'm not super social myself, but I do it. 

Online: be active on LinkedIn, find Slack and Discord communities. Find where your people are and show up consistently.

Track down alumni. "Hi, I'm a recent grad from [your school], would you have 15 minutes to share what the industry looks like from your seat?" works. "Can you refer me for a job?" does not.

2: Use AI as a tool, not a crutch. Build real fluency (not just prompting for cover letters, but building workflows, automating repetitive tasks, using it as a just-in-time learning partner). Use AI to get better and learn faster. 

3: Multi-thread your search. Don't put everything into one application, one 
company, one network. Run parallel tracks (applications, networking, learning, content). Start a side project. Do gig work. Do contracts. Take a transition job, so you have money coming in. 

Bonus: You landed the job. Now what?

Don't coast. Early career is not the time.  Keep learning, keep networking, keep the side gigs going, and keep looking. 

1: Shift from job seeking to job shopping. I'm not saying jump ship in the first six months, but now that you're employed, you can afford to be pickier (a paycheque is coming in). Plus, you're on probation for the first few months, so you're not safe yet. 

2: Outwork people. This is your window. Put in the reps. People will call it hustle culture or toxic. Let them, then out-work them. Out-learn them. Out-grow them. 

3: Be likable. Not a pushover, just be cool. You'll disagree with things at work (no job is perfect, that's why they pay you). But when the team goes for a beer after work, go. You don't have to drink. If they play pickleball and you don't play, that's fine (just show up). It will help your career in ways you can't see right now.

4: Keep multi-threading. If your side hustle worked, keep it going, do gig work, having multiple income streams will keep you safer if things change. 

Four posts, a lot of words, but hopefully useful for someone navigating this market right now. 

#jobseeking #hiring #newgrads #recruiting #earlycareer #students

🚀 Many CFOs repeat the same concerns about AI adoption. Some are reasonable, others outdated, and a few actually increase risk or block value from tech investments.
Here are 6 common “AI in finance” worries, how to reframe them, and what to do instead 👇

1)“AI isn’t safe for financial data.”
The biggest risk isn’t AI itself – it’s employees using free, unmanaged tools with company data (“shadow AI”). The question isn’t “allow AI or not?” – it’s “govern it or not?”. Most companies already have their financial data in the cloud; what’s missing are clear rules and controls.

2) “Hallucinations mean the tech isn’t ready.”
Hallucinations are often a context problem, not a tech problem. Give AI what a good analyst has: prior years, contracts, policies, CoA notes, board materials. Control the data sources your AI can access. Better context and reliable sources → fewer hallucinations and more useful outputs.

3) “What happens to junior analysts?”
Hiring is changing: you’re no longer hiring one person, you’re hiring a person plus their ability to design AI‑enabled workflows. The new baseline is orchestrating outputs at scale, not just building one report or model.

4) “Prompt engineering is everything.”
Prompt tricks are overrated for daily work. They matter most in project instructions, reusable “skills”, and scheduled workflows. For everything else, talk to AI as you would to a strong analyst: be clear on goals, constraints, and expected format.

5) “Longer context windows change the game.”
When AI can hold years of data and instructions in context, a single well‑designed prompt can replace many manual steps. With governed access to historical data, AI agents can surface reliable insights or execute standard tasks with limited human intervention.

5) “Let’s block AI.”
Blocking AI usually increases risk. People will still use it, just outside your governance and visibility. You don’t remove AI from your organization; you only remove your ability to guide and control it.

✅ The CFO choice isn’t AI vs no AI.
It’s AI with your rules and governance vs AI without your visibility.
A practical approach:
Audit what’s already happening
Approve one (or a few) secure tools + roadmap
Publish a clear AI policy (allowed data, owners, review)
Build a culture with a shared understanding of risks and opportunities

#CFO #Finance #AI #Governance #RiskManagement #Accounting #FP&A #DataSecurity

The Market Is Moving — Just Not in a Straight Line

Req Volume Up, Placements Lag, Operational Pressures Mount

Every month, TechServe Alliance convenes 60+ IT & Engineering staffing and solutions executives to talk about what's actually happening in the market — pipelines, margins, operations, and strategy. Not sentiment. Ground truth.
In April, executives shared a market that looks flat on paper but feels very different underneath.

Our April IT Employment Index confirms it: IT employment added just 209 jobs month-over-month in March — effectively flat. But that's the lagging indicator. What our Executive Roundtable participants are experiencing tells a more nuanced story.

Several signals stood out:

📍 Volume is up, starts lags — Job order volumes are rising across the board, but actual starts are trailing that increased volume. Clients are hiring more deliberately, with fewer exploratory requisitions and more quality-focused requirements. The "A" priority jobs as a ratio of overall reqs has jumped sharply while average spread margins remain under pressure.
📍 Candidate fraud is now a frontline challenge — Across multiple groups, fewer than half of candidates tested are passing fraud screening and skills verification. Verification tools are becoming embedded in workflows — but adoption within recruiting teams is uneven and requires active internal champions. Firms not thoroughly verifying candidates may win in the short-term, but will pay a price in the long-term.
📍 Margin pressure is compounding from multiple directions — Spread compression, rising insurance and compliance costs, and growing AI/SaaS tool expenses are converging to squeeze P&Ls. Firms without a clear financial operations strategy are quietly falling behind.
📍 Delivery models are evolving fast — Firms are deploying AI across sourcing, screening, intake generation, and sales outreach. The emerging posture: fast follower — try quickly, short contracts, discard what doesn't work.
📍 Sales motion is shifting — Several firms are hiring salespeople outpacing the hiring of recruiters, reflecting a conviction that sales and business development — not candidate sourcing — remains an ongoing challenge. Referral strategies and deeper account penetration are winning over new logo chasing.
The firms building structural advantages right now are deploying AI with discipline, verifying quality, and deepening client relationships. The "wait and see" window on AI adoption is closing.

AI's Impact on Real Estate That No One Is Questioning

We're not in a "jobs are changing" moment.

We're in a "the entire contract between work, place, and life is being rewritten" moment. And nobody's connecting the three threads that matter most.

Thread 1: AI isn't coming for whom you think.

The top 3 jobs getting hit hardest right now? Software developers (20% decline in early-career employment since late 2022 — per ADP/Stanford). Customer service reps. Accountants and financial analysts. Not factory workers. Not truck drivers. The white-collar knowledge workers who thought they were safe.

Good news: It's not 90% or 50% of white-collar jobs eliminated. BCG estimates that 10-15% of jobs will be eliminated outright within 5 years. Another 50-55% will be fundamentally reshaped. McKinsey says 40% of the workforce needs reskilling within 3 years. But here's the part nobody's saying out loud: the new jobs being created pay a fraction of the ones disappearing. A displaced $180k product manager driving Uber at $45k is technically "employed." Technically.

Thread 2: An entire generation is opting out of... everything traditional.

South Korea's fertility rate hit 0.75 in 2024 (it ticked up to 0.80 in 2025 — still the lowest on Earth). Japan just hit record-low births — a number demographers didn't expect until 2041. India has dropped below replacement fertility. In the US, GenZ marriage rates keep falling.

53% of skilled GenZ professionals already freelance. A full solopreneur tech stack now costs $3k-$12k/year — that's a 95-98% cost reduction from hiring a team. This generation isn't "struggling to find work." They're building a different model entirely.

Thread 3: Now look at real estate through this lens.

A generation that isn't getting married, isn't having kids, works as AI-augmented solopreneurs, and moves every 18 months doesn't need a 30-year mortgage. Or a downtown office lease. Or a suburban 3-bedroom.

Home values in San Francisco are already down. Seattle. Austin. The $13 trillion US mortgage market was built on one assumption: stable 30-year income. AI just made that assumption a question mark.

Meanwhile, data centers are the new commercial real estate gold rush — $400B+ in investment and growing.

The future isn't "work from home." It's "work from anywhere, for yourself, with AI as your team."

And almost nothing in our economic infrastructure is built for that.

The question isn't whether this shift is happening. It's whether we'll adapt the systems — mortgages, office design, career ladders, city planning — before they break.

What's your read? Are we adapting fast enough?

KYNDRYL JUST LAUNCHED A "SOVEREIGNTY READINESS ASSESSMENT." 18 MONTHS LATE. AND THE NUMBER THEY DROPPED CONFIRMS THE ENTIRE THESIS.

83% of business leaders now say sovereignty matters MORE than a year ago.

Read that again. Eighty-three percent. The same executives who signed Azure contracts in 2024 are now hiring assessors to map their dependencies. The market just confirmed what builders have been saying since 2024: control over the AI stack is no longer a "nice-to-have" — it is operational risk.

Kyndryl's framing is correct: "Sovereignty is no longer a theoretical or policy discussion. It is an operational risk issue organizations need to address now."

But the assessment-as-product approach has a structural ceiling. Here is the gap most readiness frameworks miss.

A sovereignty assessment tells you WHERE you are exposed. It does not tell you HOW to close the exposure. The closing requires architecture decisions: which layer do you own, which do you rent, which do you abandon entirely. That decision cannot be outsourced to an assessor — it has to be made by the team that will live with it for the next decade.

The pattern from GDPR enforcement (2018-2020) is instructive. Enterprises that bought GDPR readiness assessments spent EUR 200K-500K, got beautiful gap reports, and discovered six months later that the report did not survive contact with their actual data flows. The institutions that built privacy-by-design architecture from the ground up — fewer of them — passed audits with notably less drama.

Sovereignty will be the same arc. The assessment market will boom in 2026-2027. The architecture market will quietly grow underneath it. The enterprises that recognize the difference between "knowing you have a problem" and "having built the layer that solves it" win the next cycle.

After 25 years in banking and telecom, every regulatory wave produces the same two camps. The assessed and the architected. One pays consulting fees. The other ships compliant systems.

If you are running a sovereignty assessment this quarter — what is the architectural commitment that comes after the gap report?

We build agentic AI systems for companies. Not chatbots. Not copilots. Agents that actually do the work.

To show you what that means, here's something small I built on Sunday afternoon: a Polymarket trading bot that scans sports markets for mispricing and shorts the gap. 

Cashed its first winner Sunday night. 
Up 9.2% in 48 hours of launching.

30 minutes to build. No code. Runs in the cloud. Reports to Slack. Writes every trade to Airtable so it compounds.

The bot isn't the point. The point is what "30 minutes" means.

Two years ago, building this required hiring a developer, wiring up APIs, building a database schema, setting up monitoring, and babysitting it for weeks. Now Hyperagent is the dev team, Airtable is the database, Slack is the dashboard.

If a "fun experiment" looks like this, imagine what we're building for clients at scale:

Agents that triage and respond to customer tickets
Agents that run billing exception resolution
Agents that scan procurement contracts for savings
Agents that qualify inbound leads before a human sees them
Agents that own a function end-to-end and report up

Not as a feature in your SaaS. As an operator on your team.

If you've been telling yourself "we don't have the engineering capacity for this," you're running on 2023 assumptions. The toolchain collapsed. The question isn't "can this be built?" It's "what would you hand off if you trusted an agent to own it?"

DM me. 

Happy to show you what we've built for other ops teams.

Full Time Contract Opportunity
Fully Remote

Senior Backend Python Developer

● Expert-level Python (3.10+) with strong knowledge of async/await, decorators, and advanced programming constructs

● Strong experience with SQLAlchemy, Alembic, PostgreSQL, Firestore, and BigQuery

● Strong expertise in prompt engineering, RAG workflows, and AI safety mechanisms
● Experience with GCP (Vertex AI, BigQuery, Cloud Run, Firestore, Secret Manager) and Azure (OpenAI, Functions, Key Vault)

● Experience with Docker, containerization, and cloud-native deployments
● Strong experience in performance tuning, scalability, and distributed systems

Nice to Have:

● Experience with streaming systems (Kafka, Pub/Sub, RabbitMQ)

Responsibilities:

● Architect cloud-native applications on GCP and Azure
● Build secure, high-performance APIs and data pipelines
● Implement AI safety, governance, and compliance frameworks
● Manage deployments, monitoring, and system reliability
● Collaborate with cross-functional teams including frontend, DevOps, and data teams
● Drive architecture decisions, mentor engineers, and ensure delivery quality
